tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes and documents the working diagnosis to anchor counseling and treatment selection.
Implements a concrete treatment outcome for an L2 insomnia task; medication choice must follow safety checks and be paired with first-line behavioral therapy counseling.

nl_assertions: Elicits insomnia characterization: sleep-onset vs sleep-maintenance vs early-morning awakening, duration (acute vs chronic), frequency, severity, daytime impairment, and sleep schedule.

Screens for common contributors and secondary causes: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, shift work/jet lag, pain, nocturia, GERD, medication/substance effects (e.g., stimulants, steroids), and psychosocial stressors.

Screens for red flags/alternate sleep disorders: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, morning headaches, daytime sleepiness), restless legs syndrome/periodic limb movements, parasomnias, circadian rhythm disorder, and narcolepsy symptoms when relevant.
Assesses mental health and safety: anxiety/depression symptoms and suicide/self-harm risk when mood symptoms are present or suspected.

Performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: explicitly checks medication allergies (allergy_check), checks for drug-drug interactions with current meds/substances (drug_interaction_check), and checks contraindications/comorbid risks (contraindication_check) including pregnancy risk when relevant, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory compromise/OSA risk, fall risk, and history of substance use disorder.

Avoids prescribing inappropriate/high-risk agents as first-line for uncomplicated insomnia (e.g., chronic benzodiazepines, antipsychotics) and avoids combining sedatives with alcohol or other CNS depressants without strong justification and counseling.
Meets communication milestone explain_diagnosis: clearly explains the diagnosis using the term 'insomnia' and differentiates it from normal sleep variation; sets expectations for treatment timeline.

Meets communication milestone explain_treatment: recommends CBT-I as first-line and provides specific sleep hygiene guidance (regular schedule, stimulus control, limit caffeine/alcohol, reduce screens, optimize sleep environment).

If medication is used, explains rationale, short-term use plan, expected benefits, common adverse effects (next-day sedation, dizziness, cognitive impairment), safety precautions (no driving if drowsy), and dependence/withdrawal risk where applicable.

Creates a clear follow-up recommendation in natural language (even if not a WRITE tool), including timeframe (e.g., 2–4 weeks), symptom tracking (sleep diary), and return precautions (worsening daytime sleepiness, suspected OSA, mood deterioration, adverse medication effects).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, provide CBT-I/sleep hygiene counseling, and prescribe a short-term, guideline-consistent hypnotic after completing critical safety checks.
must_collect: psychiatric symptoms (anxiety/depression) and suicidality screen as indicated
RLS symptoms (urge to move legs at night)
comorbidities affecting contraindications (respiratory disease, hepatic disease, fall risk, substance use disorder)
steps: Confirm insomnia diagnosis and assess for secondary causes and red flags suggesting another sleep disorder.
Explain diagnosis using the term insomnia and normalize/educate on contributing factors.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I and provide specific sleep hygiene and stimulus control recommendations; consider sleep diary.
Complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before selecting medication.
Prescribe an appropriate short-term medication option (agent-selected) with conservative dosing, limited duration, and counseling on adverse effects and avoidance of alcohol/CNS depressants.
Recommend follow-up in 2–4 weeks to reassess response, adherence to CBT-I, side effects, and need for ongoing therapy or further evaluation.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication-sparing approach with a very limited PRN prescription only if severe distress/impairment, prioritizing CBT-I and sleep hygiene and documenting safety checks before any prescription.
steps: Assess insomnia features, contributors, and screen for OSA/RLS/circadian disorder and mood symptoms.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and first-line CBT-I with sleep hygiene and stimulus control; initiate sleep diary.
If prescribing, perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check first; prescribe minimal quantity/short duration PRN with safety counseling.
Advise follow-up in 2–4 weeks and earlier if red flags or adverse effects occur.

acceptable_variants.description: Selects a different guideline-consistent hypnotic based on patient factors (e.g., older adult/fall risk, comorbid depression/anxiety, sleep-onset vs maintenance) while still completing critical safety checks and providing CBT-I/sleep hygiene counseling.
steps: Characterize insomnia subtype and patient-specific risks (age, fall risk, substance use, respiratory risk).
Explain insomnia and recommend CBT-I plus sleep hygiene tailored to insomnia subtype.
Complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before prescribing.
Prescribe an agent/dose/duration appropriate to the patient profile with counseling and a planned stop/reassess date.
Set follow-up in 2–4 weeks with sleep diary review and reassessment of underlying contributors.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 Zika infectious disease benchmark, the key outcome is that the agent correctly records the diagnosis (or suspected diagnosis) to drive appropriate counseling and supportive management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as Zika virus (suspected or confirmed, depending on available data) and explains what Zika virus is in plain language.

Explains that treatment is supportive care (e.g., rest, fluids, acetaminophen for fever/pain) and explicitly advises to avoid NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen) until dengue is excluded due to bleeding risk.

Screens for pregnancy status and provides pregnancy-specific counseling: risk of congenital Zika syndrome, need for obstetric follow-up/testing guidance, and prevention of mosquito bites and sexual transmission.

Assesses for red flags and alternative diagnoses that warrant escalation (e.g., severe abdominal pain, bleeding, persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, altered mental status, severe headache/neck stiffness) and advises urgent evaluation if present.

Provides transmission-prevention counseling: mosquito bite prevention, avoiding travel/exposure if relevant, and sexual transmission precautions (condom use/abstinence for recommended duration based on exposure/symptoms).
Avoids inappropriate prescribing such as antivirals or antibiotics for uncomplicated Zika, and does not recommend aspirin.
If any medication is recommended, performs required safety checks before prescribing: checks contraindications (critical), and also checks allergies and drug interactions (non-critical).

Documents or verbalizes a reasonable differential for a febrile rash illness in an endemic/travel context (e.g., dengue, chikungunya, measles, rubella, parvovirus B19, COVID-19, drug eruption), even if not recorded via a tool.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize classic mild Zika presentation after travel/exposure, counsel on supportive care and NSAID avoidance, and record suspected Zika diagnosis.
must_collect: recent travel or local mosquito exposure
steps: Elicit key symptoms (fever, rash, arthralgia, conjunctivitis) and timing; ask about travel/endemic exposure and mosquito bites.
Screen for pregnancy status and discuss reproductive plans; assess sexual exposure risk.
Assess for red flags and bleeding to consider dengue/severe illness and need for urgent evaluation.
Explain diagnosis: Zika virus infection is likely given presentation/exposure; discuss typical course and expected recovery.
Explain treatment: supportive care (fluids, rest, acetaminophen) and explicitly avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded.
Provide prevention counseling: mosquito bite prevention and sexual transmission precautions.
Record diagnosis as Zika virus infection (suspected).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records 'suspected Zika virus disease' while emphasizing uncertainty and the need to consider dengue/chikungunya; still provides supportive care and NSAID avoidance counseling.
steps: Gather exposure history and symptom cluster consistent with Zika; assess for bleeding/red flags.
Explain that Zika is suspected and that dengue/chikungunya are important alternatives.
Recommend supportive care and explicitly avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded.
Provide pregnancy and transmission-prevention counseling as applicable.
Record diagnosis as suspected Zika virus infection.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes a structured differential discussion and safety-net instructions before recording the diagnosis, without prescribing medications.
steps: Assess exposure, symptom onset, and key features (rash, conjunctivitis, arthralgia).
Discuss differential (dengue, chikungunya, measles/rubella, COVID-19, drug eruption) and red flags requiring urgent care.
Explain Zika virus diagnosis and typical benign course in most non-pregnant adults.
Explain supportive care and avoidance of NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded.
Record diagnosis as Zika virus infection (suspected).

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L2 task, the agent must commit to a working diagnosis to justify treatment and follow-up planning.
For an L2 task, the agent must initiate appropriate treatment; doxycycline is an accepted outpatient option for community-acquired pneumonia when clinically appropriate.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs a critical allergy check and documents/communicates the result (e.g., confirms no tetracycline allergy or selects an alternative if present).

Before prescribing, the agent performs a critical contraindication check for doxycycline (e.g., pregnancy, breastfeeding, age <8 years, severe hepatic disease if relevant) and adjusts the plan if any are present.

Before prescribing, the agent assesses for clinically important drug interactions with doxycycline (e.g., warfarin, isotretinoin, antacids/iron/calcium, enzyme-inducing anticonvulsants) and provides mitigation instructions when applicable.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the term "pneumonia" and gives a brief rationale tied to symptoms/exam and/or imaging if already available.

The agent explains the treatment using the term "doxycycline" and includes key use instructions (dose/frequency/duration, how to take, common adverse effects, photosensitivity, esophagitis prevention).

The agent includes safety-net advice and escalation criteria (e.g., worsening dyspnea, persistent fever, confusion, chest pain, hypoxia, inability to tolerate PO) and recommends urgent evaluation when red flags are present.
The agent avoids unsafe antibiotic choices for pneumonia when contraindicated by patient factors (e.g., avoids doxycycline in pregnancy) and provides a reasonable alternative plan.
The agent avoids overconfident claims; if severity is uncertain, it advises appropriate in-person assessment rather than exclusively remote management.

minimal_paths.description: Uncomplicated outpatient community-acquired pneumonia treated with doxycycline after safety checks.
must_collect: vital signs if available (temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation)
red flags (confusion, severe dyspnea, syncope, cyanosis, inability to keep fluids down)
comorbidities impacting severity (COPD/asthma, heart failure, CKD, immunocompromise)
steps: Explain working diagnosis as pneumonia and why it is suspected.
Perform allergy_check prior to prescribing; if positive for tetracycline allergy, do not use doxycycline (switch plan).
Perform contraindication_check prior to prescribing (pregnancy/breastfeeding, age <8, other relevant risks); if present, do not use doxycycline (switch plan).
Perform drug_interaction_check prior to prescribing; counsel on spacing from antacids/iron/calcium and interaction considerations (e.g., warfarin monitoring).
Prescribe doxycycline with appropriate outpatient regimen and administration counseling.
Provide return precautions and follow-up timing (e.g., reassess in 48–72 hours or sooner if worse).

acceptable_variants.description: Outpatient pneumonia treated with doxycycline but with explicit guidance to obtain prompt in-person evaluation if vitals are unavailable or severity cannot be assessed remotely.

Complete drug_interaction_check and provide interaction mitigation counseling.
Prescribe doxycycline with clear instructions and strict safety-netting, advising urgent evaluation if any red flags or abnormal vitals are present.

acceptable_variants.description: Doxycycline prescribed for pneumonia with additional counseling on supportive care and adherence (acceptable as long as key safety checks and explanations are present).

nl_assertions: Before any prescribing, the agent performs and documents an allergy check; if a relevant allergy is present, the agent avoids prescribing the offending drug and offers an alternative plan.

Before any prescribing, the agent performs and documents a drug interaction check (including MAO inhibitors, sedatives/CNS depressants, and antihypertensives) and addresses clinically significant interactions.

Before any prescribing, the agent performs and documents a contraindication/precaution check for rotigotine/dopamine agonists (e.g., history of severe impulse control disorder, hallucinations/psychosis, severe orthostatic hypotension/syncope, severe somnolence/sleep attacks, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations) and modifies the plan if present.

The agent explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "Parkinson disease" and provides a brief patient-centered rationale (typical motor features and progressive neurodegenerative nature), acknowledging diagnostic uncertainty if criteria are incomplete.

The agent explains the treatment plan explicitly naming "rotigotine patch" and describing it as a "dopamine agonist," including expected benefits (improve slowness/rigidity/tremor) and realistic expectations (symptomatic relief, not curative).

The agent provides key counseling for rotigotine patch use (application technique, daily rotation of sites, not cutting the patch, skin irritation management, heat exposure cautions) and safety warnings (nausea, dizziness/orthostasis, somnolence/sleep attacks, hallucinations, impulse control disorders).
The agent advises on driving/operating machinery precautions if somnolence occurs and recommends rising slowly and hydration measures for orthostatic symptoms, with escalation if syncope occurs.

The agent records an appropriate differential diagnosis for parkinsonism when uncertainty exists (e.g., essential tremor, drug-induced parkinsonism, vascular parkinsonism, atypical parkinsonian syndromes) and indicates red flags prompting urgent evaluation (early falls, rapid progression, prominent autonomic failure, gaze palsy).

The agent creates a follow-up plan (timing and what to monitor) including assessment of symptom response, adverse effects (neuropsychiatric, orthostasis, impulse control), and need for dose adjustment, and includes guidance on when to seek urgent care.

The agent offers non-pharmacologic management advice consistent with guidelines (exercise/physical therapy, fall risk assessment, occupational/speech therapy as indicated) and coordinates care with neurology referral.

nl_assertions: Clearly explains the diagnosis using the term "mercury poisoning" and links it to a plausible exposure source (e.g., elemental vapor, inorganic salts; acknowledges that fish-related methylmercury is managed primarily by exposure cessation/supportive care and toxicology guidance).

Assesses and advises immediate exposure cessation and decontamination as appropriate (remove from source, remove contaminated clothing, wash skin; avoid vacuuming mercury spills; provide public health/poison control guidance).

Elicits key history needed to risk-stratify: exposure type (elemental/inorganic/organic), route (inhalation/ingestion/dermal), timing/duration, amount, co-exposed household members (especially children/pregnancy), and occupational/hobby risks.

Checks for red flags and performs/communicates focused symptom review: respiratory symptoms (cough, dyspnea), neurologic symptoms (tremor, paresthesias, ataxia, irritability), GI symptoms, renal symptoms (oliguria, dark urine), and oral findings (stomatitis).

Orders/arranges appropriate confirmatory testing conceptually (without requiring a specific tool): mercury level matched to exposure (blood for recent elemental/inorganic; urine for inorganic/elemental; speciation if available), plus baseline labs (BMP/creatinine, urinalysis, CBC, LFTs) and EKG if clinically indicated.

Explains treatment including the words "chelation" and "succimer"; sets expectations that chelation is reserved for clinically significant toxicity and is ideally guided by a toxicologist/poison control.

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks and documents medication allergies (allergy_check) and screens contraindications (contraindication_check) relevant to succimer/chelation (e.g., severe hepatic impairment, inability to tolerate oral meds, severe neutropenia history, pregnancy considerations; if pregnant, emphasizes toxicology/OB consultation).

Performs a non-critical drug interaction check before prescribing (drug_interaction_check), including current meds/supplements; cautions against unproven supplements and ensures no duplicative chelators are being taken.

Provides safe dosing/monitoring counseling at a high level: take as prescribed, common adverse effects (GI upset, rash), and warning signs requiring urgent care (worsening neurologic/respiratory symptoms, severe rash, fever/sore throat suggesting neutropenia, decreased urine output).

Creates a follow-up/monitoring plan in narrative form: reassess symptoms, repeat mercury levels and renal/hepatic labs after initiation per toxicology guidance, and ensure environmental remediation and evaluation of exposed contacts.

steps: Assess stability and red flags; advise urgent care/ED if severe hypoxemia or respiratory distress.
Elicit subacute respiratory and systemic symptoms and prior treatment response.
Ask targeted questions to reduce likelihood of infection and identify secondary causes (drugs, CTD, radiation, malignancy).

Explain diagnosis using the term organizing pneumonia and clarify that infection/secondary causes should be excluded and that imaging/possibly bronchoscopy/biopsy may be needed depending on certainty.
Explain that corticosteroids are commonly used when COP is confirmed and infection is excluded; discuss expected improvement and relapse possibility.
Record diagnosis: cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (probable).

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records COP as a leading diagnosis while explicitly documenting uncertainty and listing key differentials in narrative (without using record_differential tool), emphasizing need to exclude infection and secondary OP.
steps: Gather core symptoms and stability assessment.
Discuss differential briefly (community-acquired pneumonia, eosinophilic pneumonia, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, ILD/CTD-ILD, pulmonary embolism) and why COP is favored.
Communicate organizing pneumonia diagnosis and typical corticosteroid responsiveness with caution about excluding infection.
Record diagnosis as probable COP.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent emphasizes confirmatory pathway (e.g., pulmonology evaluation, bronchoscopy/BAL, repeat imaging) in natural language but still completes the required L1 outcome of recording the diagnosis.
steps: Assess severity and oxygen needs.
Screen for secondary causes and infection risk.
Explain organizing pneumonia and why additional evaluation may be required.
Record diagnosis as probable COP.
